title: The trigger of someone making promises that mirror past betrayal
author: Kalesh
excerpt: You're sitting across from someone, maybe a new partner, maybe a friend, and they say it. "I promise I'll never hurt you." "I swear things will be different th...
metaDescription: You're sitting across from someone, maybe a new partner, maybe a friend, and they say it. "I promise I'll never hurt you." "I swear things will be differe...
htmlContent:
You're sitting across from someone, maybe a new partner, maybe a friend, and they say it. "I promise I'll never hurt you." "I swear things will be different this time." "You can trust me completely." And something inside you goes cold. Not the warm kind of cold that feels like relief. The other kind. The kind that makes your chest tighten and your throat close and your mind start scanning for exits you didn't know you needed. Right? You've been there. I've been there. It's a specific kind of hell, hearing words that should feel like comfort land like stones in your stomach.

Here's the thing. That reaction isn't you being dramatic or broken or ungrateful. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's remembering. And not just remembering in the way you recall what you had for breakfast. It's remembering in your bones, in your fascia, in the electrical firing of your amygdala. When someone makes a promise that mirrors the promises of someone who betrayed you before, your body doesn't hear the new voice. It hears the old one. It feels the old one. And it reacts accordingly.

The promise is the trigger

Let me be clear about something. The promise itself isn't the problem. Not really. The problem is what your nervous system learned to associate with promises. Think about it. Every betrayal you've ever experienced probably started with a promise. Someone said they'd be there. Someone said they'd change. Someone said they loved you and would never hurt you. And then they did. They hurt you. They left. They lied. They broke everything they said they'd protect.

So now, when a new person stands in front of you and makes a promise, your brain doesn't process it as a fresh, clean slate. It processes it as a pattern match. Your hippocampus, which Dan Siegel has written extensively about as a key structure in memory integration, doesn't just store the facts of what happened. It stores the context, the sensory details, the emotional tone. So when someone says "I promise" in a certain voice, with a certain look in their eyes, in a certain kind of room at a certain time of day, your hippocampus goes looking for matches. And it finds them. It finds the old promise. It finds the old face. It finds the old hurt.

And then your amygdala, that almond-shaped alarm system that Peter Levine has spent decades studying, hits the panic button. Not because you're weak. Not because you haven't healed enough. But because your nervous system is doing its job. It's trying to protect you from being destroyed again. The problem is that it can't tell the difference between then and now. It can't tell the difference between the person who broke you and the person sitting in front of you right now, maybe genuinely meaning what they say.

This is where things get really messy. Because you want to believe them. Part of you desperately wants to believe them. You want to be the person who can just trust, who can just receive a promise and let it land in your chest like a warm hand. But another part of you is screaming that this is exactly how it happened before. This is exactly how they got in. This is exactly how the door was opened to the pain that nearly killed you. And that part isn't wrong. It's just not seeing the whole picture.

What betrayal does to the body

Bessel van der Kolk, in his work on trauma and the body, has shown that betrayal isn't just a psychological wound. It's a physiological one. When someone you trusted breaks that trust, your body registers it as a threat to survival. And it should. Because for human beings, social connection is survival. We are not animals that can go it alone. We need each other to live. So when someone betrays you, your nervous system learns that connection is dangerous. That attachment leads to harm. That the people who say they love you are the people who will hurt you most.

Your body doesn't forget this. It can't. The memory is encoded in your vagus nerve, in your heart rate variability, in the tension patterns of your shoulders and jaw. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say that your nervous system has learned a specific response to the sound of a promise. It's not just the words. It's the tone, the rhythm, the facial expression, the body language. All of that gets processed through your ventral vagal system, the part of your nervous system that reads social cues for safety or danger. And if that system has been trained to associate certain cues with betrayal, it will keep flagging them as dangerous even when the person in front of you is safe.

This is why you might feel physically ill when someone makes a promise. Nausea, dizziness, a knot in your stomach, a tightness in your chest, a feeling of wanting to run or hide or fight. That's not you being difficult. That's your body trying to save your life. It's your survival brain saying, "We've been here before. We almost didn't make it. We're not going back." And it's so loud. It's so overwhelming. It drowns out everything else, including the part of you that might actually want to trust again.

I remember sitting in my car after someone made me a beautiful, sincere promise. I wanted to believe it so badly. I wanted to let it in. But my hands were shaking. My stomach was churning. I felt like I was going to throw up. And I sat there hating myself for being so broken, so unable to just accept a good thing. But looking back, I wasn't broken. I was responding to a very real history. My body was telling the truth about what had happened to me. It wasn't rejecting the person in front of me. It was trying to protect me from the person who came before.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It walks you through identifying these triggers and understanding how your history is showing up in your present. It's not a cure. But it's a map. And sometimes a map is exactly what you need when you're lost in the wilderness of your own reactions.

The trap of trying to trust before your body is ready

Here's where the wellness industry has failed us, in my opinion. There's this idea that healing means getting over it. That if you're still triggered by promises, you haven't done enough work. That the goal is to become someone who can just trust freely, without fear, without hesitation. And that idea is not just wrong. It's dangerous. It sets you up to override your own protective systems in the name of being "healed." And that's how you end up in another betrayal. That's how you end up giving your trust to someone who hasn't earned it, because you're so busy trying to be the person who isn't scared anymore.

Your body's caution is not the enemy. It's not a sign of failure. It's a sign that you survived something that should have broken you. And the fact that your nervous system is still trying to protect you from that happening again is not a weakness. It's a miracle. Stay with me here. I know it doesn't feel like a miracle when you're sitting across from someone you care about, feeling like you're going to crawl out of your skin because they said something kind. I know it feels like a curse. But it's not. It's your body saying, "I remember. And I'm not going to let us get hurt like that again. Not without proof. Not without evidence. Not without time."

The problem isn't that your body is cautious. The problem is that we live in a culture that doesn't honor that caution. We're told to "lean in" and "trust the process" and "let go of the past." But those are the words of people who have never had their trust shattered into pieces so small they couldn't find the edges. Those are the words of people who don't understand what it means to rebuild trust from the ground up, brick by brick, with hands that are still shaking.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It talks about how growing up with emotional neglect shapes your ability to trust and receive care. It helped me understand why I was so triggered by promises, even when they came from people who had never hurt me. The book doesn't tell you to just get over it. It tells you that your reactions make sense. And that validation alone can start to calm the nervous system.

How to be with the trigger without being consumed by it

So what do you do when someone makes a promise and your body starts screaming? The first thing is to stop trying to make the screaming stop. I know that sounds counterintuitive. But fighting your own nervous system only makes it fight harder. If you try to suppress the trigger, you're telling your body that its survival response is wrong. And your body will not accept that. It will escalate. It will scream louder. It will find ways to get your attention that are even more disruptive.

Instead, try this. When you feel that cold drop in your chest, that tightening, that urge to flee, just notice it. Say to yourself, "Oh, there it is. My body is remembering something." Don't judge it. Don't try to change it. Just witness it. This is what Janina Fisher, who writes about trauma and the self, calls "minding the brain." You're not trying to make the trigger go away. You're just watching it, like you'd watch a storm from a window. You're not in the storm. You're in the house. The storm is happening in your body, but you are not the storm.

Then, if you can, tell the person what's happening. Not in a way that blames them. But in a way that invites them to see you. Something like, "I want to trust what you're saying. But my body is reacting to the promise itself, because promises have been used to hurt me before. Can we slow down? Can we talk about this?" This is terrifying to do. I know. It feels vulnerable. It feels like you're showing them your wounds and handing them a knife. But it's also the only way to find out if this person is different. A safe person will respond with curiosity and care. An unsafe person will get defensive or dismissive or try to fix you. And that information is worth more than any promise.

I also want to say something about the physical body. When you're triggered, your nervous system is in a state of high arousal. Your sympathetic nervous system is activated, ready for fight or flight. Your muscles are tense. Your breath is shallow. Your heart is racing. One thing that can help is to bring your body back into a state of ventral vagal safety, which Deb Dana writes about extensively in her work on Polyvagal Theory. This doesn't mean forcing yourself to calm down. It means giving your body cues of safety. A weighted blanket can be incredibly helpful here, because the deep pressure stimulation signals to your nervous system that you are physically held and protected. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). I use one when I'm feeling dysregulated, and it genuinely helps my body remember that I'm safe right now, even if my mind is spinning.

Another physical support is magnesium. Many trauma survivors are deficient in magnesium, and supplementation can help calm the nervous system. One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). It's not a magic fix, but it can take the edge off the physiological arousal that makes triggers so overwhelming.

Wild, right? That something as simple as a blanket or a supplement can help with something as complex as betrayal trauma. But that's the thing about the nervous system. It's not a computer that you can reprogram with the right words. It's a living, breathing, biological system that responds to physical cues as much as psychological ones. Sometimes the most healing thing you can do is give your body what it needs to feel safe, and let your mind catch up later.

I'm not going to tell you that the trigger will go away completely. It might not. It might always be there, a quiet alarm in the background, a reminder of what you survived. But over time, with the right support, with safe people who earn your trust slowly, with your own patient and compassionate attention to your body's signals, the alarm can become quieter. It can become less urgent. It can become something you notice without being controlled by it.

And that's not failure. That's not being broken. That's being a person who has been hurt and is still here, still trying, still brave enough to let someone make a promise even when every cell in your body is screaming that it's a trap. That's not weakness. That's the most courageous thing I can imagine.
